title: Research Practice Throughput Score: Where Studies Actually Stall
subtitle: A 17-question diagnostic for founders, research directors, and COOs running boutique qual and quant practices with 10 to 40 staff. Score 0 to 100. Higher means healthier throughput.

audience.locations: the US, UK, Australia, and Canada

methodology_anchor.name: Agent-Ready Ops
preconditions: Structured input data. Briefs, screeners, transcripts, and prior reports live somewhere queryable, not buried in inboxes and one-off Google Docs.
Documentable decision logic. The rules a senior analyst follows when coding a transcript or stress-testing a screener can be written down explicitly.
Narrow initial scope. The first automation handles one stage of one study type, not a sweep across qual and quant at once.
Human-in-the-loop by design. Every synthesis output, recommendation, or client-facing slide is reviewed by a human before it ships.
methodology_anchor.intro: This assessment scores your practice against the four preconditions for Agent-Ready Ops. Hit all four on a given handoff and that handoff is ready for AI to carry weight. Miss one and any AI build there will leak hours back.

proof_card.sample_top_leak: transcript prep and coding (about 12 hours recoverable per study)

persona_selector.text: Which best describes your research practice today?

answers.label: Solo or 2 to 5 staff. Founder still does most fieldwork and synthesis.
6 to 15 staff. Early boutique with one or two senior leads running studies in parallel.
16 to 30 staff. Growing practice with defined research director, ops, and IC roles.
31 to 50+ staff. Established mid-market practice running multiple concurrent engagements across qual and quant.

questions.text: How consistently does every new study start from a structured intake brief that captures objectives, decisions the client needs to make, and success criteria?

answers.label: 1. Briefs are ad hoc. Each PM writes them differently or skips them entirely.
2. We have a template but most studies kick off from a client email or call notes.
3. Template is used about half the time. Quality depends on who is leading.
4. Every study has a completed intake brief in a known location before fielding.
5. Intake briefs are required, reviewed by a second person, and linked to the project record.

questions.text: How disciplined are you at catching and pricing scope creep mid-study (extra interviews, added segments, late-breaking questions)?

answers.label: 1. We absorb almost everything. We rarely raise it with the client.
2. We notice creep but only push back when it is severe.
3. PMs flag creep informally. Pricing conversations happen sometimes.
4. We have a written change-order process and use it on most studies.
5. Scope deltas are tracked in the project record and trigger an automatic client conversation within 48 hours.

questions.text: Score the quality of the kickoff handoff from sales or partner to the delivery team.

answers.label: 1. Delivery often learns about a sold study with no written context.
2. There is a verbal handoff but key context lives in the seller's head.
3. We hold a kickoff call. Notes are inconsistent.
4. Standard kickoff doc covers objectives, stakeholders, sample, and timeline.
5. Kickoff produces a project record the delivery lead signs off on before fielding starts.

low.headline: Your studies are starting blind. That is the upstream leak.
low.text: When intake is unstructured, every downstream stage pays the cost. The fix here is not a tool. It is a single shared brief template that becomes a non-negotiable gate before fielding. Until that exists, scope creep, screener rework, and report rewrites are guaranteed.
steps: Write a one-page intake brief covering objectives, decisions to inform, audience, sample plan, and success criteria.
Make completion of the brief a hard gate. No screener work starts until it is signed off.
Audit the last five studies against the new template and note where each would have caught earlier confusion.

mid.headline: The template exists. Adoption and rigor are the gap.
mid.text: Honest take: you have the right artifact but enforcement is uneven, which means the leak shows up unevenly across PMs. Tighten the gate and add a peer-review step so quality stops depending on who is leading the study.
steps: Require a second person to review every intake brief before kickoff. Five-minute review, not a full edit.
Add a scope-change clause to every brief and reference it the first time creep appears.
Track briefs in your project tool so you can see at a glance which studies skipped the gate.

high.headline: Intake is dialed. Sharpen the link to delivery.
high.text: Keep sharpening. The frontier here is connecting intake structure to the synthesis stage so that objectives written at kickoff are auto-surfaced when the analyst sits down to write the report.
steps: Tag each research question in the brief with a unique ID and carry that ID through to coding and report sections.
Build a kickoff-to-readout traceability check that flags any objective that did not appear in the final report.
Run a quarterly review of intake briefs against actual deliverables to catch drift early.

operator_brief.headline_number: About 4 to 6 hours per study recoverable when intake is gated and standardized
operator_brief.lever: Make a one-page intake brief the non-negotiable gate before any fielding work begins.
talking_points: Most rework downstream traces to ambiguity that was already present at kickoff.
A peer-reviewed brief is the cheapest insurance against scope creep we are absorbing.
Standard briefs are the prerequisite for any AI-assisted synthesis later. Without them, automation has nothing clean to read.
operator_brief.proof_of_concept: Run the next two new studies through a one-page brief template with a mandatory second reviewer and compare hours-to-fielding against the prior two.

questions.text: How systematically do you reuse and version-control screeners across similar studies?

answers.label: 1. Every screener is built from scratch in a fresh doc.
2. We copy from the most recent similar study, ad hoc.
3. We have a folder of past screeners but no metadata or tagging.
4. Screener library is tagged by methodology, audience, and category.
5. Library is searchable, versioned, and the team starts every screener from a tagged base.

questions.text: How rigorous is your QA before a screener goes live (logic checks, terminations, quota tests)?

answers.label: 1. The PM reviews their own screener and we ship it.
2. A second person reads it. No structured test plan.
3. We click through the screener manually before launch.
4. Standard QA checklist with logic, quotas, and edge-case test paths.
5. Documented test plan plus dry-run respondents before live launch on every study.

questions.text: How automated is your in-field monitoring for fraud, speeders, straight-liners, and panel quality issues?

answers.label: 1. We trust the panel and check at the end if quotas look off.
2. PMs eyeball completes once a day during fielding.
3. Manual quality checks at fielding milestones.
4. Standard fraud/speeder rules applied across studies, reviewed daily during fielding.
5. Automated daily quality dashboards flagging anomalies before the PM logs in.

low.headline: You are rebuilding the same screeners and missing fielding leaks.
low.text: Building screeners from scratch is the most expensive habit in a boutique practice. The fix is library-first construction plus a real QA checklist. This category is where AI assistance pays off fastest, but only after the library exists.
steps: Tag the last 20 screeners by methodology, audience, and category and store them in a single searchable location.
Build a one-page screener QA checklist covering logic, terminations, quotas, and edge cases.
Require that every new screener starts from a tagged base in the library.

mid.headline: Library exists. The QA layer is where hours are still leaking.
mid.text: My read: your team is reusing assets but the QA layer is informal, which means errors get caught in field rather than before launch. A documented test plan with dry-run respondents catches roughly twice as many issues as click-through QA alone.
steps: Convert your QA process into a written test plan that lives in the project record.
Run two to three dry-run respondents through every screener before live launch.
Add automated daily quality checks for speeders and straight-liners during fielding.

high.headline: Screener and fielding ops are tight. Push toward predictive QA.
high.text: Keep sharpening. The frontier is moving from reactive QA to predictive flags, where panel and respondent patterns trigger alerts before quotas fill with low-quality completes.
steps: Build a fielding dashboard that scores partial completes for quality in real time.
Define termination logic that auto-flags suspect respondents for review rather than rejecting silently.
Run quarterly post-mortems on rejected completes to refine fraud rules.

operator_brief.headline_number: About 3 to 5 hours per study saved with a tagged screener library and a written QA checklist
operator_brief.lever: Move screener construction from blank-page to library-first with a non-negotiable QA test plan before launch.
talking_points: Every from-scratch screener is a hidden tax on PM time and an avoidable risk in field.
Click-through QA misses roughly half the issues a structured test plan catches.
A tagged library is the input layer that makes screener-assist tooling actually useful later.
operator_brief.proof_of_concept: Tag the last 20 screeners in a single folder this week and route the next two new studies through a library-first build with a written QA checklist.

questions.text: How automated is the path from raw audio or video to clean, speaker-labeled transcripts ready for analysis?

answers.label: 1. Manual transcription or outsourced with multi-day turnaround.
2. Auto-transcript tool, but heavy manual cleanup every time.
3. Standard transcription tool with a documented cleanup process.
4. Automated pipeline producing speaker-labeled transcripts within hours.
5. Transcripts auto-routed into the analysis workspace with metadata attached, no human handoff.

questions.text: How disciplined is the prep step on quant data before analysis (data cleaning, recoding, weighting)?

answers.label: 1. Each analyst cleans data their own way. No documentation.
2. Cleaning happens but lives in undocumented Excel or SPSS work.
3. We use a standard checklist, applied inconsistently.
4. Cleaning scripts or templates are reused across similar studies.
5. Versioned, auditable cleaning pipeline tied to each project record.

questions.text: How quickly can a senior analyst find every transcript, dataset, and stimulus from a study six months later?

answers.label: 1. We have to ask whoever ran it, and even they may not remember.
2. Things live across drives, email, and shared folders. It takes most of a morning.
3. Every project has a folder, but structure varies by PM.
4. Standard project folder structure across all studies.
5. Project record links every artifact and is retrievable in under five minutes.

low.headline: Prep is where studies silently lose the most hours.
low.text: Manual transcript cleanup and ad hoc data prep are the single biggest avoidable cost in a boutique practice. The fix is a consistent pipeline that delivers analysis-ready inputs without an analyst hand-cleaning every time.
steps: Pick one transcription tool and standardize on it across the practice.
Document the post-transcription cleanup steps and cut anything that does not change the analysis.
Adopt a single project folder structure and back-fill the last 10 studies into it.

mid.headline: Pipeline exists but is still touching too many human hands.
mid.text: Honest take: the steps are there but each one still requires a person to babysit the handoff. Automating the routing, not the work itself, is usually the highest-leverage move next.
steps: Auto-route transcripts from the transcription tool into the project workspace with metadata attached.
Templatize quant data cleaning into reusable scripts or flows tied to study type.
Add a 30-day audit catching projects that did not follow the standard folder structure.

high.headline: Prep is industrialized. Now extract more from your archive.
high.text: Keep sharpening. The frontier is treating your past transcripts and datasets as a reusable asset for new pitches and meta-analysis, not just historical files.
steps: Index past transcripts so themes can be queried across studies.
Build a re-use layer that surfaces relevant prior findings during new study scoping.
Track how often archived data informs a new pitch and use that as a proxy for archive ROI.

operator_brief.headline_number: About 8 to 12 hours per study recoverable with a clean transcript and data prep pipeline
operator_brief.lever: Standardize on one transcription pipeline plus one project folder structure so analysts never hand-clean or hunt for assets again.
talking_points: Manual transcript cleanup is the largest single time leak we can name in current operations.
Inconsistent folder structures cost senior analyst time every time we revisit a study.
Clean prep is the precondition for any AI-assisted coding or synthesis to be trustworthy.
operator_brief.proof_of_concept: Run the next study end-to-end on a single transcription tool with a documented cleanup checklist and a fixed folder structure. Time the prep stage against the prior study.

questions.text: How consistently do analysts apply a defined coding framework across qualitative studies?

answers.label: 1. Each analyst codes by feel. No shared framework.
2. We discuss themes in a debrief but do not code formally.
3. We use a coding framework on flagship studies only.
4. Standard coding framework applied across most studies.
5. Versioned coding frameworks per study type, with inter-coder reliability checks.

questions.text: How predictable is synthesis time per study (from final transcript to draft findings)?

answers.label: 1. Synthesis time is wildly variable. Some studies double their estimate.
2. We estimate but routinely overrun by 30 to 50 percent.
3. Estimates are roughly accurate on standard study types.
4. Synthesis time is predictable within 15 percent on most studies.
5. Predictable within 10 percent and tracked as an internal KPI.

questions.text: How much leverage do senior analysts have on synthesis (junior coders, AI-assist, structured templates) versus doing it all themselves?

answers.label: 1. Senior analysts do nearly every code and every theme themselves.
2. Some delegation to juniors, mostly on simple tasks.
3. Juniors handle initial coding. Senior reviews and synthesizes.
4. Senior analyst time is concentrated on synthesis and client framing.
5. Senior analyst output per week has measurably increased through structured leverage.

questions.text: How easily can any team member trace a finding in a final report back to the underlying transcript or data point?

answers.label: 1. Trace requires asking the analyst who wrote it.
2. Some studies link evidence. Most do not.
3. Quotes are pulled into a working doc, but back-linking is manual.
4. Each finding links to source quotes or data in a structured way.
5. Full traceability from finding to source, queryable across past studies.

low.headline: Synthesis depends on heroes. That caps the practice.
low.text: When coding is by feel and synthesis time is unpredictable, every study runs at the senior analyst's personal capacity. The fix is a documented coding framework before any AI-assist tooling. Without that framework, AI in synthesis amplifies the inconsistency.
steps: Document the coding framework currently in your senior analyst's head for your three most common study types.
Pilot the framework on the next two studies with a junior doing first-pass coding.
Track synthesis time per study so variance becomes visible.

mid.headline: Framework exists. Leverage and traceability are the next unlock.
mid.text: My read: the framework is doing its job, but senior analysts are still the bottleneck on synthesis itself. The next move is structured leverage (juniors plus templates plus selective AI-assist), with traceability built in so client trust does not drop.
steps: Templatize synthesis output for common study types so analysts fill structured slots, not blank pages.
Pilot AI-assisted theme extraction on coded transcripts with a senior analyst as final reviewer.
Require evidence links from every claim in a final report back to source quotes or data.

high.headline: Synthesis is leveraged. Push into cross-study insight.
high.text: Keep sharpening. The frontier is treating coded findings across studies as a queryable asset, so a new pitch can be informed by every relevant prior engagement in minutes, not days.
steps: Build a cross-study theme index over your last 24 months of coded work.
Pilot AI-assisted retrieval on that index for new pitch prep and proposal scoping.
Measure win-rate change on pitches that pulled prior insight versus those that did not.

operator_brief.headline_number: About 6 to 10 hours per study reclaimed when senior analysts have real synthesis leverage
operator_brief.lever: Document the coding framework that lives in your top analyst's head, then build leverage and traceability on top of it in that order.
talking_points: Synthesis variance is the single biggest drag on margin per study right now.
AI-assist on synthesis without a documented framework is the fastest way to ship inconsistent findings.
Evidence traceability is the trust layer that lets us safely accelerate without losing client confidence.
operator_brief.proof_of_concept: Pick one study type, write the coding framework explicitly, and run the next two studies of that type with junior first-pass coding plus senior synthesis review. Compare hours and rework.

questions.text: How standardized is your final report format across study types?

answers.label: 1. Every report is built from a blank deck or doc.
2. We have a few templates but PMs deviate frequently.
3. Templates exist per study type and are used most of the time.
4. Templates are required and updated quarterly based on client feedback.
5. Templates are componentized. Sections are reused with traceable evidence links.

questions.text: How predictable and controlled are internal review cycles before client delivery?

answers.label: 1. Reviews happen at the last minute and often delay delivery.
2. Standard review exists but timing slips routinely.
3. Reviews are scheduled and usually meet timing.
4. Review cycles are owned by named roles with clear sign-off criteria.
5. Review is a defined gate with structured feedback captured against the report record.

questions.text: How well do you reuse delivered reports, frameworks, and findings to inform future studies and pitches?

answers.label: 1. Once delivered, reports rarely get opened again internally.
2. Senior analysts remember relevant work and dig things up manually.
3. We have a folder of past reports. Searching is hit or miss.
4. Past work is tagged and searchable by industry, methodology, and theme.
5. New pitches and studies are routinely informed by retrieval across our archive of past work.

low.headline: You are rebuilding the wheel on every report.
low.text: Blank-deck report building is the most visible time leak to any senior analyst. The fix is component-level templates per study type plus an owned review cycle. This is the area where most boutiques can recover days, not hours.
steps: Componentize the last five strong reports into reusable section templates per study type.
Assign a named owner for the internal review cycle on every project.
Set a sign-off gate that explicitly checks evidence traceability before client delivery.

mid.headline: Templates exist. Leverage from past work is where you are leaving value.
mid.text: Honest take: the report-building stage is in shape, but past deliverables are sitting in archive without being reused for new pitches and studies. That archive is one of your most underused assets.
steps: Tag every delivered report by industry, methodology, audience, and key themes.
Build a retrieval habit into the proposal stage so prior relevant work surfaces by default.
Track how often archived work is referenced in new pitches as a leading indicator of leverage.

high.headline: Delivery is leveraged. Push the archive into a strategic asset.
high.text: Keep sharpening. The frontier is making the archive queryable across themes and methodologies, so retrieval-assisted scoping becomes a real competitive edge rather than a nice-to-have.
steps: Index reports semantically so themes can be queried across studies, not just titles.
Build a retrieval interface that fits the actual workflow of pitch teams and senior analysts.
Run a quarterly review on win-rate lift attributable to archive retrieval.

operator_brief.headline_number: About 5 to 8 hours per study saved with componentized report templates and a controlled review cycle
operator_brief.lever: Treat report assembly as component reuse with a named review owner, not as a fresh build every time.
talking_points: Blank-deck report building is the most visible cost to senior analyst time on every project.
Last-minute reviews are the largest single cause of missed delivery dates this quarter.
Our archive of past reports is sitting unused. Retrieval is a fast unlock on pitch quality.
operator_brief.proof_of_concept: Componentize the last three strong reports into reusable sections this week and require the next two reports to be built from those components with a named review owner.

ctas.headline: Want a personalized Loom breakdown of your RPT score?
ctas.description: Drop your email and I'll review your exact answers, stack them against research practices your size, and send a 5-minute Loom within 48 hours with the ONE handoff I'd fix first. Async, no scheduling pain, no pitch. If we both see signal, we book a working call from there.

ctas.headline: Solo or 2 to 5 staff? You're early for a custom audit.
ctas.description: Automation ROI at your scale is real, but payback windows are longer and the leverage points are different. Follow me on LinkedIn. I post tactical research and insights ops breakdowns several times a week. When you cross 6 staff and start running studies in parallel, come back and retake. The CTA will change.

ctas.headline: You're running tight. Here's what research practices your size miss next.
ctas.description: At your score, the obvious leaks are closed. The next gains come from cross-study theme retrieval, AI-assisted synthesis with full evidence traceability, and turning your archive into a pitch-acceleration asset. I'm writing teardowns on each.

ctas.headline: Get the weekly research and insights ops teardown
ctas.description: One tactical case study plus one n8n workflow breakdown per week. How mid-market boutique research practices scale without scaling payroll. No fluff, no pitch.
